title: Mesopotamia
description: Test your knowledge of ancient Mesopotamian civilization!

assessment_items.question_content:
**The name _Mesopotamia_ comes from Greek and means "between two rivers".  Which are the two rivers referenced in the name?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The flooding regions of the Nile created a relatively thin greenbelt; settled agriculture in Kush and Egypt happened very near the riverbanks.

choices.content: The Tigris and Euphrates rivers in modern-day Turkey, Syria, Kuwait, and Iraq
The White Nile and the Blue Nile in Uganda, South Sudan, Sudan, Ethiopia, and Egypt
The Yangtze and the Yellow River in China

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is the significance of Sumerian cuneiform writing?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Cuneiform was used for more than religious literature; it was used to record business transactions, as well as personal writing.

choices.content: It was one of the first written scripts, and it influenced writing in Mesopotamia for two millennia
It was primarily used to write religious literature
It led to widespread literacy among Sumerian people

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did the _Code of Hammurabi_ reflect the social hierarchy of ancient Babylonia?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Elites still had to abide by laws, just a slightly different set of laws.

choices.content: Punishments in the Code were adjusted depending on the status of the perpetrator and the victim
Hammurabi's Babylon was an egalitarian society; all punishments were delivered equally
Laws were only applied to slaves and common people; the elites could do whatever they liked

assessment_items.question_content:
**How does writing help historians and archaeologists understand the history of trade in Mesopotamia?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Ancient trade documents are more like business ledgers than journals; only the most essential information is included.

choices.content: Ancient traders wrote detailed accounts of their travels and transactions
Clay tablets with cuneiform inscriptions were used as receipts for purchases, enabling us to get a glimpse into what people traded in the Fertile Crescent
A large number of written works exist from this period, and some contain references to trade

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which best explains the Mesopotamian practice of placing _ziggurats_ at the center of their city-states?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Ziggurats were where many ceremonial events were held; feasts and sacrifices were held in the temple atop the structure.

choices.content: Leaders valued religion and wanted a prominent place to pay tribute to local gods
Mesopotamians believed that demons could not reach the center of a circle, so they put the Ziggurat as close to the center as possible
The stepped architecture was designed to designate different levels of social hierarchy
